data.title: Quantum Computing: High Price For An Unproven Business Model
data.description:
Quantum computing is quite an interesting space for investors. This futuristic technology could lead to revolutionary breakthroughs across a number of fields including drug discovery, weather forecasting, financial modeling, and cybersecurity among many others.

I've been following the quantum computing space for a while. In fact, I wrote about one of the industry's first movers, IonQ (IONQ), back in 2021. At the time, I stated that:

"[IonQ] could be a pioneer in a blockbuster industry, but there's a long road between here and eventual commercial success."

I'd say that four years out, this remains true. IonQ, for its part, generated $43 million of revenues in its fiscal year 2024, which was up dramatically from the $2 million that it brought in back in 2021. However, IonQ still has a tiny revenue base in comparison to its market capitalization or the potential total addressable market size of the quantum computing market.

And though IonQ still has a fairly modest line of business at the present time, it does appear to be well ahead of many of the other publicly-traded peers in its industry.

For example, take Quantum Computing Inc. (NASDAQ:QUBT), hereafter referred to as "QCi". In its most recent quarter, QCi generated a grand total of $61,000 in revenues. This figure fell from the $183,000 of sales reported in the same period of 2024.

Despite the lack of tangible commercial traction for the company's products, that hasn't stopped traders from bidding up QUBT stock dramatically. Indeed, shares are up more than 2,000% over the past year:

This has been a remarkable comeback for a company that had appeared to be adrift for quite a while.

Per the company's 10-K, the firm was founded under the name Ticketcart back in 2001. In 2009, the company pivoted and renamed itself Innovative Beverage Group Holdings. In 2018, the company moved from beverages to quantum, renaming itself Quantum Computing Inc. Then, in June 2022, the company merged with QPhoton, Inc., which gave it much of the underlying intellectual property tied to its current approach involving the commercialization of photon-based quantum computing solutions.

I mention this backstory since companies with a history of name changes and business model pivots often tend to underperform. That said, we shouldn't discount the company's efforts out of hand simply due to its past history.

Quantum Computing's Current Strategy

Quantum Computing believes that its photon-based approach to providing quantum solutions differentiates it from the competition. It's generally viewed that it will take many years for other quantum approaches, such as trapped-ion systems, to reach a sufficient level of performance that will attract large amounts of mainstream clients rather than just niche and specialty use cases.

QCi states that its photon-based approach may allow it to ramp up revenues and deliver real-world solutions faster than many of its quantum computing peers.

In the company's Q2 earnings press release, interim QCi CEO Dr. Yuping Huang stated that:

"We delivered meaningful commercial progress in the second quarter with new customer wins across quantum sensing, cybersecurity, and AI. Our first shipments to leading research institutions and commercial enterprises in Europe, Asia, and the U.S., together with our deepening engagement with NASA, underscore the growing demand for QCi's photonic technologies and mark an important step in our commercial journey."

Huang went on to elaborate that the launch of the company's chip foundry will allow it to fulfill orders more quickly and build on the company's commercial momentum across multiple end markets.

This all sounds great on paper. However, I come back to the fact that the company generated just $61,000 in revenues in Q2, a figure which was down sharply on a year-over-year percentage basis.

For me, QCi is a prove-it story. If the company is really making significant inroads with key customers such as NASA along with in AI, cybersecurity firms, and leading research institutions, this should be reflected by a sharp jump in revenues in coming quarters. If that happens, I would give the company's current business model a lot more credit. For now, however, the lack of significant tangible revenues despite all the talk of imminent commercialization gives me pause.

Why Are QUBT Shares Trading At The Present Valuation?

Short sellers might look at QCi's $2.5 billion market cap compared to its lack of revenues and think that this is an obvious bearish target. And indeed, about 17% of the company's stock has been sold short as of this writing.

Despite the heavy bearish involvement in the company, however, QUBT stock has held up. And several of the company's quantum computing peers also continue to trade at what seem like unusually high valuations as compared to their fundamentals.

I think there a couple of factors that have been instrumental in keeping QCi shares up here in the double-digits.

First of these is that the company has a very strong balance sheet. With the most recent equity raise, the company's cash pile jumped to $349 million as of June 30th. The firm's total assets are now $426 million. By contrast, it has just $30 million in total liabilities, and just $4 million of those are current liabilities. This is about as clean of a balance sheet as you'll find for a small publicly-traded company.

Overall, QCi has more than $2/share in net cash. That's not nearly enough on its own to justify the current $16 share price. But it is plenty to stave off any near-term liquidity concerns.

To quantify that, the company has lost $18.5 million from operations through the first six months of the year, implying a roughly $37 million per year operating loss run-rate. If losses continue at that pace, the company's cash would last roughly nine years before being exhausted. And if QCi can achieve even modest commercial interest in its photon-based quantum solutions, that could extend the runway dramatically.

There's also the fact that QUBT stock continues to trade at a premium valuation. As such, it wouldn't be at all surprising if management issues more stock and further adds to its cash pile. Every time QCi issues stock around today's share price, it is adding more to its book value and cash per share figures, which reduces the amount of potential near-term downside on the share price.

And QCi seems to be somewhat risky to short due to borrow dynamics. Currently, the stock is reasonably available to borrow, at least at Interactive Brokers (IBKR) where I trade. And the borrow cost is at 5% per year, which is not a prohibitive level for a short position. However, on several occasions throughout 2025, borrow has dried up, potentially forcing buy-ins at inopportune times. And the borrow cost has spiked far into the double-digits annualized range on multiple occasions.

There is always the alternative of buying put options. However, not surprisingly given the aforementioned borrow dynamics, puts have high implied volatility.

For example, January 2026 $15 strike puts are going for about $2.75 as of this writing. To double one's money on that position, QUBT stock would have to drop to around $9.50 within about four months. That doesn't seem like a particularly great risk-reward dynamic from my perspective. If QCi shares slowly drift lower over time, put buyers would probably lose money due to time decay while a straight short sale could lose due to borrow cost and the possibility of getting bought-in during squeezes.

QUBT Stock Bottom Line

I would not consider buying shares of QCi at this time. While the company's talk of imminent commercialization may seem promising, I would need to see a lot more in the way of tangible revenues before taking a swing here. A greater than $2 billion market cap is a huge sum for a firm that is just barely beyond being a pre-revenue stage company.

For investors that do want a position in the quantum space, I would strongly consider doing so via an ETF, such as the Defiance Quantum ETF (QTUM), which owns some pureplay smaller quantum companies but also has exposure to a lot of bigger tech firms that stand to incrementally benefit as quantum computing starts to gain more steam.

I have a sell rating on QUBT stock today because shares seem dramatically overvalued in comparison to where the company's operations are today. That said, I am not at a strong sell, and I would be cautious with trying to short the company outright. The company's large cash balance and limited burn rate give QCi many years to try to grow the business and find a path to profitability. While QUBT stock may not fall nearly as much as bears expect in the near-term, however, I do expect shares to end up trading lower over the next 12-24 months, thus reinforcing a sell rating today.

data.title: Build-A-Bear Q2 Review: An Amazing Turnaround Story (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Build-A-Bear (NYSE:BBW) just reported outstanding numbers, and the company’s stock skyrocketed on a double beat and a raise in guidance. This is why I decided to have a look at the company’s progress, because it’s been over two years since I covered it last and gave it a buy rating, which outperformed the broader index by over 140% since then.

I wanted to take a look at the company’s most recent numbers and give some quick thoughts on the future and a fair value estimate, which tells us that there is still quite a lot of room for growth.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, sales came in at around $124m for the quarter, up $11m y/y, beating analysts’ estimates by around $8m. Looking deeper into the company’s revenue breakdown, Net retail sales accounted for most of the sales, coming in at $114.6m, up almost 11% y/y, while e-commerce demand within the DTC segment saw 15% growth y/y. Commercial and international franchise revenue reached $9.6m, an increase of 15.2% y/y.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability, retail gross margins came in at 57.6% an increase of 360bps over last year, due to a combination of reduced promotional activity, some cost leverage, and selective price increases. Operating margin expanded to 12.3%, up around 200bps from last year. And Diluted EPS came in at $0.94, an increase of almost 47% y/y. Q2 GAAP EPS beat analysts’ estimates by a decent margin of $0.20.

Looking at the company’s financial position, BBW finished the quarter with around $39m in cash and equivalents, against zero in long-term debt. This is a great position to be in, no matter what kind of operational performance the company exhibits. Being cash-rich and having no debt obligations in tough times is good, as it allows it to weather the downturn, and being cash-rich and having no debt when operations are going as well as they can go is even better. The company can focus on expanding its business without worrying about paying interest on debt and reducing its capital that could be used for growth.

On top of these results, the management went ahead and provided an update on the rest of the year. They are confident in the company’s operations, so they raised their guidance. They are now expecting mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth, and pre-tax income to be in the range of $62-$70m. Furthermore, the company is targeting to open at least 60 new partner-oriented locations globally, while maintaining capex in the range of $20-$25m. Digital transformation continues to be at the forefront of the company’s growth strategy, as well as its continual global expansion.

Overall, a double beat and a raise were received extremely well by the investing community, sending the company’s shares up over 14% on the day. In my opinion, this was a very good quarter. The company has been firing on all cylinders for a while now, and investors flocked to the company and raised its price accordingly.

Comments on the Outlook

In terms of the rest of the year, the outlook seems to be quite positive, with several key growth drivers to push the company higher. I am not at all surprised that essentially the same growth drivers remain when I last covered the company over two and a half years ago.

Global Expansion and Store Growth

The company looks to be even more aggressive in opening new locations, which I am a big proponent of. There are still a lot of places where it can open a store, especially outside of the US. Opening stores through franchising is the way to go and will be the most cost-efficient strategy in my opinion, since the partner-oriented model reduces capital intensity, while also leveraging local market expertise, which should help it achieve scalable growth a lot faster. There are plenty of opportunities abroad, and I would like to see it pursue growth even more aggressively. The Latin American region could use a lot more love, since, from what I can see, the company has some presence in Mexico and Colombia only.

Digitalization efforts are paying off

Back in 2023, this was my main point of growth for the company. It looks like it still remains the key growth driver going forward. 15% y/y growth is good, especially in such a niche product, so continuing to enhance the user experience on their website should translate to better overall growth and better profitability metrics. Continued investment in omnichannel capabilities and digital marketing will enhance customer engagement and surely drive online sales. The company has been quite active on social media, achieving notable success through some viral marketing campaigns this past year. The presence across the most popular social media sites has millions of followers, and the recent trend of targeting the growing “kidult” market, comprising teens and adults, tends to bring in high-value collectors, as well as viral product buyers.

Recently, the company had viral and integrated campaigns that drove a lot of clicks, like the Pumpkin Kitty, driving hundreds of millions of impressions. The company is leveraging TikTok to create a lot of buzz for the brand, and it seems to be working. It is only a matter of time before another new product takes over the internet and drives sales. One of the best ways to market such a product is through social media, and they should continue to do so.

New product launches to keep things fresh

This type of strategy worked for many other similar novelty stores. A prime example would be Miniso (MNSO). There is a lot of opportunity for the company to make new partnerships with whatever is out there currently that is culturally very popular. On top of that, why stop there? The company should continue to create new products and IPs of its own to attract more customers, but I do believe collaborations with popular brands, characters, and franchises will be the way to attract more customers and drive incremental sales and expand market appeal.

Continue work on margin expansion

I believe there is still a lot of work management can do to make the company more profitable. Going down the asset-light model is going to be the one catalyst that will help its operations improve, in my opinion. According to the company’s historical profit margins, these have been quite stagnant for the last three years or so, after improving massively from the bad years before. I would like to see how these develop over the next couple of quarters.

Valuation

As I said, it has been over two years since I did a valuation analysis on the company, so let’s dig right in.

For the whole model, I’m going to keep it simple, not too optimistic or conservative. So, for revenues, I went with a 5% CAGR for the next decade.

For margins, some improvement is achievable over the next decade, too.

For the DCF analysis, I went with the company’s WACC as my discount rate and 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. Below are all the assumptions.

We can see that the company’s fair value based on these conservative estimates is around $95 a share, meaning there is around 42% upside from current valuations.

Closing Comments

It seems the company has been doing great in its turnaround efforts; I don’t think it is going to stop anytime soon. There is still a lot of growth to be had, which should end up very well for the current shareholders.

Unfortunately, I did not add the company to my portfolio back in 2023. It seems I got distracted by something. I will continue to monitor the situation and see how it all goes down in the future, but I continue to like the company’s prospects.

data.title: Taking Profits For Yield And Growth With David Alton Clark
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

David Alton Clark, The Winter Warrior Investor, thinks the market is a little bit toppy (0:30) Managing risk, taking profits (4:40) Are rate cuts priced in? (9:10) Feeling good about shorter-term bonds (12:30) How to think about high yield (14:00)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very happy to welcome back to the show the Winter Warrior investor David Alton Clark. Welcome back to Investing Experts. Welcome back to Seeking Alpha. David, great to have you as always.

David Alton Clark: Great to be here, Rena. Appreciate it.

RS: Absolutely. Talk to us about how you're thinking about the markets these days. We had a Fed meeting. We have some tariff discussions. We have NVIDIA (NVDA) reporting later today. We've had a slew of some earnings lately, but up ahead, not so much. How are you thinking about the markets these days? What are you focused on?

DAC: Well, thanks, Rena. Right now, I feel as though the market is a little bit toppy.

It's been at all time highs, and we've made new all time highs several times throughout this year already. We've had a big bounce back from the April low. The markets have gone up about 40%, I think, at this point in time.

And everything seems to be peaking somewhat, especially with the big bump we had on Friday after Powell came out and said that there's a a high probability that we're probably gonna get a 25, that's not what he said, but he pretty much opened the door to at least a 25% cut at the September 17 meeting.

Actually, just within the last weeks, I've been taking profits on a lot of my growth side of the portfolio, and NVIDIA, Micron (MU), (AMD). A lot of the ones that I had in the quality growth portfolio were up substantially, 40 to 50% on some of them.

So I've employed my risk management strategy and taken profits on those at this time and raised cash to get some dry powder in case we hit some bumps throughout the summer.

And I do think we're gonna see a little bit of some type of disappointment or volatility coming up between now and November time frame.

It's kinda happened every year so far. If you look back, it's been kind of a rough September, October, time frame in the past few years. So, I'm expecting kind of the same right now.

Everything's really stretched as far as valuations and technically at this point in time. It seems like everybody's kind of all in already as well. There's a high level of retail money that's in the market presently.

And one of the other things that kind of raises my risk management hairs is that there's a lot of IPOs have started to proceed through the market now and there's a bunch that are planned coming up.

And that's one of the signs of a money grab, if you will, for the AI boom that's going on right now. And if you look at some of the IPOs that have happened just recently, a lot of them have popped almost 100% on the day that they IPO'd, and most all of them have given all that back so far. So they're not really acting as if things are gonna be so rosy coming up.

But I'm still in there. I'm not saying sell out of everything that you have. I don't feel like we went through the 2000 and 2008, both of the downfalls there, and I was right there in the middle of it in 2000 out in Silicon Valley, working with some of the venture capitalists, qualifying their IPOs.

And I don't feel like we're there, that this is gonna be as euphoric as we were at that time. But, it is time to employ some risk management. I do still have some growth stocks. I've got Block, Boeing (BA). I just recently bought Palo Alto (PANW) and Tesla (TSLA). So I'm still in there, but more limited.

RS: Can you share with us what the risk management strategy was that made you take the profits off the table in terms of the stocks you sold? And then what made you get into the specific stocks or stay in those stocks that you're in?

DAC: Part of it has to do with how much I'm up on it. When I start feeling as though things are getting toppy and valuations are stretched and technically, the market has gone into the overbought zone.

I'll look at the ones that I have that are anything that I've got in the growth side of the portfolio. It's over 20% gain. I'll take a look at it and review all of the key factors, the reasons why I bought it. Some of them - like, I was up 50% on NVIDIA and it had gone from $100 to $160 or so when I sold it.

The reason why I have the growth side of portfolio, if you look at the methodology, it's really about creating income. So I'd look at the growth portfolios as more of an income garden where once in a while, you have to harvest the proceeds to reallocate into the income side of the portfolio.

So with 50% up on NVIDIA, everything hitting all time highs, there could be some we're heading into a seasonally rough patch usually for the market. That's why I went ahead and just took profits on it right then. 50% profit is just fine with me.

Book that in. Overall, the three years since inception, we're up 80% total return. Probably about 20% of that was due to dividends and distributions, but the other 80% of it was taking profits on the gains of the growth portfolio and then reallocating that into the income.

So when things get like this, one of the things is I shrink my number of securities. I wanna concentrate down in on my highest conviction ideas. So I'll look through there and cut back the number of positions I have.

I think I was at about 40 or so, and now I'm down to 23, especially in this type of situation. And the ones that I am holding on to are the ones that I have the highest conviction in. Normally, I'm talking when I call things in the risk management phase, it's mainly from the growth side of the portfolio.

On the income side, that's what I'm trying to build up for retirement.

So most of those, if they go down at a certain time like this, I'll use that opportunity to actually buy another tranche and increase my yield and decrease the basis on those.

But on the growth side of the portfolio, I'm gonna snap off the profits and then use that as dry powder, either enter back into some growth ideas that I see are more set up to have more upside. Like Tesla was one of my most recent buys and Palo Alto, both of those were kind of in a beaten down state even though the market is way up.

Tesla and Palo Alto in recent times had actually taken big dips down and actually bounced off the bottom, there's still an uptrend, but they're at the bottom of the uptrend channel, and they bounced off the bottom of the uptrend channel.

It's an ideal time to start a new position. Plus, I'm layering into those as part of the risk management strategy too. When I go into a new position like Palo Alto, I've got planned 41% tranches rather than plunking down the whole position, a full position in one shot.

I still got three more bullets to fire, let's put it that way, at Palo Alto to layer in and get a good basis going on those. That's kind of the way the risk management discipline works.

RS: I appreciate that. And then in terms of the seasonality you mentioned, it's known that the September season is somewhat negative, historically speaking.

Do you have a sense or how are you thinking about the proposed rate cuts coming in September? Will that maybe affect that negativity? Will it make it more positive? Or has it already been priced in, and are people assuming that that's already coming?

DAC: That's a good question. Definitely, I feel that it's priced in as far as the 25% cut coming up on the September 17 meeting.

Even a lot of the Fed speakers that have come out lately, they are substantially restrictive. Looking at where inflation is right now, and where they have the rates at 4.25 and you've got inflation at 2.7, there's room for them to cut and still be in a restrictive stance. I think there's a lot of political side of it going on right now with Trump saying he's gonna fire Cook.

So I see a lot of uncertainty, and I think they might be overpricing in what's gonna happen. I'm not sure if I think Powell's gonna do a one and done type situation. I don't think he's gonna set us up for, they're gonna cut each sequential meeting. I believe two or three cuts is actually priced into the market right now, and I'm thinking, he might just do a 25% cut at the next meeting.

And what I'm calling it is a hawkish cut to where he's not going to say, hey. You know, we're in a new paradigm, and he's gonna go and stay with his same mantra where he's like, we're data dependent.

I do see part of the reason why I took profits now is I do see some risk between now and the September 17 meeting due to the fact that we got PCE coming up on Friday. And if there's any pop in inflation on any of the upcoming data points between now and the 17th, that may even throw a monkey wrench into that 25% cut, and that could precede a sell off in the market.

That's part of the reason why I've gotten set up with some dry powder now just in case because I can see how that would happen.

A lot of people are starting to talk about stagflation, Rena, where inflation's going up and the economic indicators are are going down.

And we have had some some issues where employment is getting worse, slowly but surely. So it's not like dropping off a cliff with the way they wanna glide path in to a Goldilocks scenario.

But if anything happens to where all of a sudden there's a sharp drop in the employment and some of the economic indicators, then that could be trouble.

RS: And speaking of this part of the marketplace, there's a lot of talk about bonds in conjunction with interest rates and what the Fed will be doing and where we are economically speaking. What are your thoughts about the bond market? How would you contextualize that for investors?

DAC: I feel good about the shorter term bonds.

It's kind of interesting you just asked that question because my latest buy from Friday was the Bearings Global short duration high yield bond fund. And I started that off with a 1% position in that.

It's actually yielding 10% right now. There is a higher level of risk in bonds right now if we get into a rate cutting cycle, but the bearings global is, the ticker is (BGH), and I think that's one of the best ways to get a little bit involved in the bond market and get yield out of there.

It could be risky. It's a little riskier than normal because it is a CEF, so they use leverage. I think the bond market's gonna hold up just fine, but I'd be more interested in the shorter term bonds, two and five yield bonds because those are the ones where the the interest rates on the shorter terms are gonna hold, are more reliable and hold steady.

The long term bonds could be the ones where if we have some kind of economic trouble or something, those might fall more than the shorter term bonds.

RS: And how are you thinking about high yield vehicles? How should retail investors, investors in general be thinking about the high yield space these days?

DAC: One of my portfolios is the quality high yield portfolio. The thing about that I think a lot of people don't take into consideration is that, I see a lot of portfolios where everything's in high yield.

Like, let's say, you've got 40 positions in your entire portfolio, and they're all high yield positions, all of them, eight to 10%. I don't really advocate for that for a few different reasons.

One is that high yield itself is a class of stocks. And so if even though you have 40 high yield stocks, you're really not diversified because they're all in that one class. High yield tends to gravitate up and down, together just like all other types of stocks.

My thing is to have about 20% of your portfolio in high yield, and I like to use more in my most high conviction positions. Like, rather than buying whatever it might be, 20 or 40 high yield stocks, I'd rather have right now, I'm looking at my high yield portfolio, and I have 11. So I'd rather have my top 11 highest conviction high yield stocks in this environment rather than spreading it across a whole bunch more.

High yields, there's definitely some good high yield picks right now. The one that I just picked was BGH. And I like to pick the ones that are like BizD instead of a BDC.

I'll get the one that's a conglomerate of all those. And so I've got Starwood (STWD) and Ares Capital (ARCC). Some of the best of the best, the cream of the crop of the high yield, I would I would stick to that.

RS: I mentioned at the start of the conversation that you're The Winter Warrior Investor. That's the name of your investing group on Seeking Alpha. Do you share these portfolio selections with your subscribers there?

DAC: Yes. On the on the service there's a resources tab. I have the portfolios in the inception portfolio, as a resource link.

And then I do a a weekly macro update insight piece on Saturdays where I go through everything that happened in the past week, what I think is gonna come up the next week. And then underneath that, I'll put the current performance and the five best winners and the five biggest losers for the previous week.

Everybody's got access to all five portfolios. There's twenty, twenty four positions altogether. I've got a Super Swan portfolio, which is they range that because a lot of the service members depending on their suitability, some of them want the just the safest, they've got a lot of money, and they want something really safe and secure, and they only are looking for about 5% yield.

So I've got a Super Swan portfolio, which is where I'm really not concerned at all about capital loss with this. And the yield right now is currently, 4.7, and we're actually up 10% on that out of those, and there's only four positions in that one. And so we're at capital gain plus a 5% yield there.

Then I've got the Swan portfolio, which is actually fairly high yield. It's a 9. 2% yield. There's six positions in there. And we're also up 6% on that. So, part of the thing is, my main mantra is and this is what I learned from my dad. He was a stockbroker.

I was actually born in Omaha, Nebraska, and, my dad was kinda growing up at that time, the same time as as Warren Buffett. And a lot of the people from that area of the country are conservative. I look for income capital appreciation, but also capital preservation. There's a lot of people that they don't wanna lose their money.

They don't wanna lose money to try to to make it on just the income. So in the SWAN portfolio, it's up 6%. It yields nine points too.

Then I have the quality high yield portfolio, which actually yields 11%. There's eight positions in that one, and, we're up 3% on that one. So I haven't lost any money on any of the income side since the start of the service where and that's kind of a personal thing for me.

I really have a hard time taking capital losses even if they're unrealized. So if I'm making 10% on something, but this the the the vehicle I bought is down 50, then I feel like they're just giving me back the money that I already lost.

You know what I mean? So it's like I'm not really making any money off that. So I like to have it to where I don't lose any money on the capital side, but yet I get the dividend.

And now, that's not always true. I've had a couple of big ones that that I lost on the Icon fund. I took a hit on that. And my the issue with that, the lesson learned there was is I was a big Icon fan from the very start of my career. I just really admired the way that he made his way up from nothing himself and, became this big investing icon.

And so I kinda let that gloss over the the obvious issues with the ICON fund. So at some point in time, you've gotta cut your losses on one that you're losing in and reinvest that money into something else.

Another thing I learned over the years is you don't have to make your money back on the exact same stock that you lost it on. That used to be one of my issues when I was younger. I would just keep hanging on and hanging on and trying to to just get to a point where I got back to even. And then, normally, you would end up selling out.

And so you had a bunch of opportunity cost there because you sat there for a year or two just to get back to even just to get out of it, whereas you could have sold out and maybe pick some a better vehicle that might have gone up already.

And so when a recent example of that was I bought Pfizer (PFE) when it was at its lows, and I thought it was the lowest. It was at about, I think, 26 or 27.

I thought Pfizer's just beaten down. It's yielding 6%. So I'm gonna start a position in that, and, I think it's probably gonna make a recovery, but it actually ended up going down to, like, 20. And, so I was down about $8,000 on the vision. It wasn't very big. But, the story had changed to where I thought, okay. This isn't playing out the way I thought it would.

So instead of just hanging on to that, hoping it would make its way back up to 26 or 27, I went ahead and sold that, and I went into a speculative growth, (IONQ), one of the most speculative stocks in the quantum field.

And that one actually popped up 69% within a month or so of buying it. So I more than doubled my money back on that one, and I ended up selling that one. It was up 69. Had one big day where it really, really popped big on some news, and I decided to go ahead and clip the coupon on that.

And so I made the money I lost on Pfizer, I made it back on the Ionq in just a couple of months and then put that back into the bank and actually use that money to level up some of the income portfolios.

What I'll do from time to time is I'll go across one of the portfolios, and I'll add 15,000 or whatever 1% is. Right now, we're up pretty well now. So 15,000 was 1%. But now that we're at, 1.75, it's 1,700 and 17,500 is the 1% level. So I'll take that and and go across one of the income portfolios and just add 1% to every position to increase the income levels.

I'm about to be 62 in September. I'm still not quite there to retirement yet. So I'm still in that building phase. And as time goes on, like right now, I'm probably, like, sixty forty, with 60% on income and 40% on growth.

But as time goes along, that's why this service actually works for people that are building a retirement portfolio and for when they retire and then actual retirees because you can still use these same positions, just you're waiting.

As you get older, the methodology is that you just keep increasing the weight on the income side. So somebody that's 75 or 80 or so, they might be 80% into the income side and only have 20% in growth stocks that they're still harvesting from.

And this is something I learned from my dad and actually, one of the biggest lessons learned from that was that my dad actually passed away in 2013 and he was managing his own money. He had his own portfolio, and it was about a million dollar portfolio. He had just bought Tesla a few months before he passed away.

I started helping my mom, going through all of her finances and everything and making sure that everything was squared away as far as everything was under her control and all that kind of stuff. And that's when I really got involved in my parents' portfolio. As far as the way that my dad had done things, he had a pension and everything else. So my mom really didn't even need any of the income from the portfolio at all and she didn't need to take any risks either at that point in time.

So when I looked at it, I saw the Tesla position and at that time it was still kind of an unknown as far as how well you know, what is this? And, so I told her, I said, you should just take this money and just put it into CDs. You don't really need the income from it. That's the safest thing to do.

But,her nickname was the general. She passed away in 2019, but she told me, I'm not changing anything your dad had in there. We're leaving it exactly the way it is. I was like, okay, mom. And Tesla turned out to skyrocket over that ten year period, or from 2013 to 2019 at least.

So that's when I thought, well, hey, you should probably always have a little bit towards the growth side and speculative things in the portfolio just in case for the wealth creation aspect of it because that almost pretty much doubled my dad's portfolio over that six or seven year period.

RS: Well, you sound like a good son and somebody who knows how to learn from experience, which is I think a really important skill for an investor specifically.

Anything else, Dave, that you would share with our audience about dividend investing specifically or something to keep in mind about this time that we're in?

DAC: At this point in time, what I'm doing right now and what I've already just finished doing, but one thing I could say is that, if you don't have a lot of time to dedicate to this yourself, you probably shouldn't do it because even a dividend stock, a lot of people think, well, you know, the stocks might go up or down, but I'm always gonna get that dividend payment at the end of the day.

Well, that's not necessarily true.

If something goes bad in the stock market, the dividends might be the last thing to go. But if a stock takes a big hit, they might have to cut or completely eliminate or suspend the dividends.

So I would go through my dividend portfolio and just double check on each of the positions and make sure that you feel solid about it.

I did sell off a few things. It was with some of the PIMCO funds that I held. I went ahead and took profits on those because I was about even on those, but PIMCO had actually cut their distribution payments on a few of their funds recently.

And so they didn't cut them on the ones that I owned, but I thought, okay. Well, why don't I just do a little risk management here, and I'm up. I've collected a bunch of dividends over the past few years, and so I'm gonna go ahead and sell those and then reallocate it to the ones that I feel stronger about.

But, another thing too that you can do is, like, with ExxonMobil (XOM), I know when we first started talking, I had ExxonMobil. A long time ago, ExxonMobil was at, like, $35, and it yielded 10%.

And so I put $100,000 into that. I was like, look, I'm gonna lock in this 10% yield. And so over the next couple of years, ExxonMobil had gotten up to about $120. Big huge capital gain in that.

I was still making 10%. But what I see a lot of people do is they're just happy to have that big cushion of cap unrealized capital gains in a position so they don't have to really worry about it, losing any money.

And they're making the 10%. So they're like, I'm still making 10% on it. I've got this huge unrealized capital gains. But what another little trick I learned from my dad is, go ahead and take profits on that.

If you don't wanna sell it all, cut it back at least to all of your capital gains and then buy into something else that's making 10% currently.

ExxonMobil at that point in time, it was only if you bought more ExxonMobil, it was only yielding about 3% because of how high up it had gotten on the price. So I took that money and I bought Enterprise Products (EPD), and that was yielding 10%.

Doubled my income on that instead of just sitting on capital gains unrealized. Take some profits on that, reinvest it into something else that's making 10, and you double your income on that position.

So I see a lot of people sitting with big capital gains on their dividend positions when if you take some profits, you don't have to sell it all or you could sell it all, and then reallocate that to other ones that are yielding 10% at the time. That's a way to increase your income.

RS: Appreciate it, Dave. Again, it's David Alton Clark on Seeking Alpha.

The Winter Warrior investor is his investment group. Really, so much good stuff just on this podcast alone, I think, is enough reason to be a part of your service if this is something that you're interested as an investor.

Such good stuff, Dave. Really, thank you for taking the time and sharing so much with us. Any final words?

DAC: I'd just like to say, thanks a lot. I really appreciate everything you've done for me, Rena, and Seeking Alpha. I guess my final word would be, what I talked to about the group. We've got a great group of investors.

So if you're out there working alone, it really takes a village. If you can find an investing group or some type of group of people or friends that you can use as a sounding board, I think that's really important.

A major part of success over the last three years has been the team members and charter members of The Winter Warrior Investor that we've got a really lively chat room, and that's a big place where I can put my ideas out there before I make a buy, and then I'll hear the pros and cons of what people think about that. Other people have ideas of what they see as opportunities in the market.

So I would definitely advise anyone that's listening to this podcast to sign up for Seeking Alpha, and especially the premium product is really great. And I use that a lot for all my research and everything.

I think it takes a village is the final word, and patience equals profits. You should take your time, do really good due diligence on whatever you're about to purchase. Layer into it. There's no rush.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nu Holdings: Strong Growth, Profitable, Cheap P/E
data.description:
Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU) is a fast-growing Latin America-focused digital banking platform that has considerable customer acquisition momentum in its core business. The Fintech generated 29% top line growth in the second-quarter and the platform's net income is surging at an even faster rate. The Fintech has continual momentum in a number of Latin American countries and has potential to expand its crypto offerings in order to boost platform transaction volumes. Shares of Nu Holdings are widely undervalued given the growth profile and potential for earnings growth and I continue to recommend the Fintech as a buy following the Q2 earnings scorecard.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Nu Holdings a buy in my last work on the Fintech in May 2025 -- A Cheap Fintech Play For 2025 -- because I liked the platform's deep market penetration in the largest market in Latin America, Brazil. Nu Holding is also improving its profitability which, based off of net income, is advancing faster than the company’s top line. I believe the Fintech's shares are undervalued based off of earnings and offer investors a very favorable risk profile as well as revaluation upside.

Strong customer growth, surging profitability

Key to Nu Holdings’ success has been that the Fintech excelled in penetrating its core markets deeply and signing up a ton of customers. In the second-quarter, Nu Holdings had 123M total customers -- mainly in Brazil which is where 60% of the adult population are using the platform’s banking products and services -- meaning the company added 18M new customers in the last trailing-twelve period, or an average of 1.5M customers per month. This strong customer growth has translated to significant top line growth as well: Nu Holdings' total Q2’25 revenues were $3.7B, showing 29% growth year-over-year.

Because of its significant growth momentum in markets like Brazil, Mexico and Colombia, Nu Holdings is growing its gross profits as well. In the second-quarter, Nu Holdings’ total gross profits surged to $1.55B, showing 14% year-over-year growth and 24% on a currency-adjusted basis. About 43% of this gross profit came from the firm's credit products which are essential to both the firm's growth as well as profitability.

Nu Holdings is also widely profitable on a net income basis which is what separates the Fintech from a lot of U.S.-based Fintechs that I covered in the past. In Q2'25, Nu Holdings generated $637.0M in net income from its Fintech operations, showing 31% year-over-year growth (42% on a currency-neutral basis). This level of profitability puts the Fintech on a 2.4-2.5B annual net profit run-way in the next-twelve months period.

Levers for growth and valuation multiplier expansion

Nu Holdings has a number of catalysts in its portfolio that could help the firm accelerate its growth and unlock more value for shareholders over time:

  • Nu Holdings could expand into other Spanish-speaking markets in Latin America in a bid to diversify away from the dominant Brazilian market
  • Nu Holdings has a huge opportunity to capitalize on growing demand for credit cards which are a fast-growing product on the Fintech platform. In the second-quarter, credit card debt in Nu Holdings' portfolio totaled $18.2B, showing 27% year-over-year growth, and representing 67% of all debt investments.

  • Thirdly, Nu Holdings has a huge opportunity to move into the crypto space by allowing consumers to buy major cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin (BTC-USD), Ethereum (ETH-USD) and others. In fact, the platform’s crypto customers totaled 6.6M as of the end of the June quarter, showing 41% year-over-year growth. Rising crypto adoption and growing proliferation of digital assets could create a new opportunity for Nu Holdings to capitalize on the growth of the industry and boost its growth.

Nu Holding’s valuation profile

Nu Holdings is projected to grow its earnings the fastest in the industry group as strong customer acquisition and new services like crypto are expected to turbo-charge the Fintech's growth. Nu Holdings is currently trading at a forward P/E ratio, based off of FY 2026 earnings, of 17.0X which is widely below the price-to-earnings ratios for most U.S.-based Fintech platforms like SoFi Technologies (SOFI), Upstart (UPST) and Block (XYZ). These three Fintechs are priced at forward P/E ratios of 49.2X. 27.7X and 21.7X, with the industry group average being 28.9X.

PayPal (PYPL) -- which I did not include in the industry group -- is an outlier here because the company is a mature Fintech with serious profitability on an earnings and free cash flow basis, but the platform has become a low-growth business, especially in relation to the other Fintechs mentioned. This has resulted in a low P/E ratio of 12.1X which is not reflective of the implied growth potential of other Fintechs in the market.

In my opinion, the risk of an investment in Nu Holdings is very low because the platform maintains strong top line momentum while already being widely profitable on a net income basis. If shares of Nu Holding were merely to revalue to the industry group average P/E ratio of 28.9X, using a consensus EPS estimate of $0.77 per-share for FY 2026, I could see a fair value in the neighborhood of $22.25, implying 56% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with Nu Holdings

Nu Holdings is chiefly focused on three markets -- Brazil, Mexico and Colombia -- which is where the platform is seeing solid customer acquisition and revenue momentum. The platform will ultimately have to expand in Latin America and maybe even bring its products and services to other regions in North America, Europe and Asia which creates long term growth upside for investors. What would change my mind about Nu Holdings is if the Fintech were to see slowing gross profit/net income growth or if it failed to boost its crypto business in the years ahead.

Final thoughts

Nu Holdings is a massively undervalued Latin America-focused digital banking platform with a number of levers at its disposal to boost its growth, including the roll-out of its platform in other Latin American countries and growing its crypto business. Shares of Nu Holdings are trading at only 17.0X forward earnings and therefore the valuation embeds a significant safety margin, in my opinion. The fact that Nu Holdings is widely profitable based off of net income drastically limits risks for investors. I like the risk and the growth profile, as well as the valuation of the Fintech platform, and consider shares widely undervalued. If Nu Holdings can sustain its high rate of growth and customer acquisition, I believe shares have material revaluation potential in the long run.

data.title: Blackstone: Best Of Breed But Overvalued (Upgrade)
data.description:
In late May, I reiterated my sell rating on Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) as I expressed my view that it still wasn't time to buy yet. I cited mixed fundamentals as well as a high valuation as reasons for my bearish stance. So far, it turns out that I was wrong. The stock has gone on to advance by nearly 25% since the publication of my article, far outpacing the S&P 500's ~10% gain over this period. In the below analysis, it is shown that the company's fundamentals are now robust with strong fee related earnings growth and impressive investment performance. While there was some weakness for inflows, AUM overall saw nice increases. As for the valuation, Blackstone trades at a questionably large premium to peers but due to the fact that the fundamentals are solid, I have decided to upgrade Blackstone to a hold.

Financial Measures

Blackstone reported their 2025 Q2 earnings in late July and most financial measures looked very robust. As a whole, fee related earnings surged an impressive 31% YoY to $1.459 billion. Management and Advisory fees were a bit sluggish with growth of just 13% but of course remains the largest source of earnings. Fee related performance revenues surged a whopping 167% YoY to $472.05 million. This clearly shows that Blackstone is seeing very strong returns on their investments which is driving growth for the firm. As you can see on the right, the company saw quite healthy investment performance in their Q2. They did see some weakness in their real estate portfolio, especially in Core+, but if you compare these returns to the previous quarter or to the prior year period, you can see that appreciation/returns were highly respectable.

DE Per Share

For Q2, Blackstone reported a 'distributable earnings' per common share of $1.21, up a fairly healthy 26% YoY. This beat expectations by $0.11 to show modest outperformance. In the above chart, you can see that growth accelerated rather steeply from the previous quarter's 11% and that's of course a good sign. While Blackstone does not provide financial guidance, the above chart also shows consensus expectations for the next four quarters. Some growth moderation is expected in Q3 before a rocky end to the year in Q4. Looking further, analysts do expect a strong start to 2026 and so overall the outlook seems to be positive for the company.

Assets Under Management

Let's take a look at their AUM and flow metrics to see how strong client activity has been. For total AUM, they saw a 13% increase YoY to $1.2112 trillion. This is an acceleration from Q1's 10% increase. However, do note that in Q2, they only saw inflows of $52.1 billion versus $61.6 billion in the previous quarter. Therefore, this suggests there may be some hesitation in clients to invest more capital. For fee-earnings AUM, growth was unchanged from Q1 at 10% and now the total for this figure is at $887.1 billion. Again, however, inflows were less than in Q1 as they reported $41.9 billion for Q2 while the previous quarter figure was $44.2 billion. While there does seem to be some weakness in sentiment, their highly important perpetual capital AUM saw growth of 16%, outpacing Q1's 14% and now represents 47% of fee-earning AUM versus the previous quarter's 46%. With perpetual capital being a key long term growth driver for the company, I believe this is an encouraging sign for investors. Overall, there was some investor hesitancy in the quarter but nonetheless, AUM figures were solid. Next, I'll briefly mention some of their segment results.

Segment Performance

Real Estate

Despite some weakness in real estate investment performance, this segment still saw respectable results for the most part. Fee related earnings were up 13% YoY to $543.625 million. Some notable drivers for this increase were a decrease in expenses as fee related compensation dropped by 8% while other operating expenses dropped by 6%. This signals better efficiency in this segment. Despite the relatively weak investment performance, Blackstone still deployed $6.2 billion in capital in real estate and so it seems they are taking advantage of the sluggishness in this space. As a whole, total AUM dropped by 3% but they still had inflows of $7.2 billion as a signal of client confidence.

Private Equity

Their private equity segment knocked it out of the park. Fee related earnings grew by a staggering 87% YoY to $519.404 million. Management and advisory fees saw strong growth while fee-related performance revenues took off. If you take a look back at the company's investment performance, you can see that private equity saw strong returns/appreciation. In the quarter, they deployed $11.8 billion of capital in this segment, showing that they are continuing to make investments even if it is less than Q1's $16.4 billion deployed. Lastly, total AUM increased by 18% to $388.9 billion with inflows of $15.3 billion.

Credit & Insurance

Decent results were seen in their credit & insurance segment. Fee related earnings rose 12% YoY to $333.025 million. Efficiency of this segment was a bit on the low side with fee related compensation and other operating expenses increasing by 28% and 22%, respectively. A highlight for Q2 includes the fact that the $14.1 billion deployed eclipsed the previous quarter's $14.0 billion deployed. They stated that this was driven by U.S. direct lending and so there are signs that the economy is holding up alright so far in the country. This segment saw the largest inflows as they reported $26.8 billion. Total AUM increased by a more than respectable 23%.

Multi-Asset Investing

This segment is by far the smallest for Blackstone but results were healthy. Fee related earnings increased by 16% to $63.449 million. Operational efficiency was quite strong as other operating expenses increased by just 2% YoY. Their Absolute Return Composite gross return of 2.8% or net return of 2.5% notably outperformed the HFRX Global Hedge Fund Index's 1.8%. This segment also managed to see inflows of $2.7 billion and total AUM increased by a decent 13% YoY.

Valuation Premium Questionably Large

The P/B ratio for Blackstone and competitors, Apollo Global Management (APO) and KKR & Co. (KKR), are shown above. The P/B ratio has rebounded quite a bit for Blackstone from the lows of earlier this year and is at a major premium to peers. Firstly, as a whole, I believe it is clear that Blackstone's fundamentals are currently solid. However, are they so much better such that the valuation premium is justified? Currently, Seeking Alpha rates Blackstone's growth as an A- versus Apollo's C+ and KKR's B+. As for profitability, Seeking Alpha rates Blackstone a B+ while Apollo is rated an A- and KKR a B+. Overall, according to these ratings, Blackstone does seem to have the stronger fundamentals but I would have to say that the P/B ratio premium does seem to be a bit excessive currently relative to peers and so the risk/reward setup for Blackstone doesn't seem all that attractive currently. That said, with the fundamentals of the company being rock solid, I do believe I was overly pessimistic with my sell rating earlier this year.

Concluding Remarks

For a good part of this article, it was shown that Blackstone's fundamentals are solid after overall impressive Q2 results. Fee related earnings were strong and distributable earnings were robust as well. While there was some weakness in inflows, AUM nonetheless saw nice increases as their investment performance was more than respectable. That said, as stated above, the large P/B premium to Apollo and KKR is questionable as Seeking Alpha ratings show that Blackstone is the better company but not by a major margin. KKR has B+ ratings for both growth and profitability but trades at a 70% discount. Therefore, in terms of a risk/reward perspective, those two companies may present a better opportunity for investors. Nonetheless, the strength in their Q2 results show that I was likely overly bearish in my previous article and thus I have decided to upgrade Blackstone to a hold.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Pulls Back
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 3.8 percentage points to 34.6%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the fifth time in nine weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 1.6 percentage points to 26.0%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 58th time in 60 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 5.4 percentage points to 39.4%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 39th time in 41 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 9.1 percentage points to –4.8%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 28th time in 30 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how their sentiment toward the stock market has changed since the start of the year.

Here is how they responded:

  • I’ve become more bearish: 32.1%
  • I’ve become more cautious, but not bearish: 33.3%
  • It is about the same: 18.7%
  • I’ve become more bullish: 15.5%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 34.6%, up 3.8 percentage points
  • Neutral: 26.0%, up 1.6 percentage points
  • Bearish: 39.4%, down 5.4 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: New GDP Numbers: What Do They Show?
data.description:
The GDP numbers for the second quarter of 2025 have been revised and they have been revised upwards.

Year-over-year...the measure that I prefer to annualized quarterly results...came in at 2.1 percent for the second quarter of 2025.

This is still down from preceding years, but still above a 2.0 percent level.

In the second quarter of 2024, the U.S. economy had risen by 3.0 percent over the second quarter of 2023.

And the number for the second quarter of 2023 was 2.8 percent. For the second quarter of 2022, the economy grew by 2.5 percent year-over-year.

So, since the second quarter of 2021, the U.S. economy has been growing at a fairly strong pace, above 2.0 percent.

This period of time has been dominated by the "new" monetary policy of the Federal Reserve system, the program devised and implemented by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke.

Let's increase the picture here back to 2009, when Bernanke's program was put into place.

During the 2010's the Federal Reserve conducted three rounds of "quantitative easing," which resulted in a pretty steady rate of growth in the U.S. economy with an annual growth rate of a little more than 2.5 percent.

In 2020, the COVID-19 pandemic hit the economy, and the Federal Reserve responded with another round of "quantitative easing," this time under Fed Chair Jerome Powell. This round injected a "massive" amount of reserves into the banking system. Chairman Powell wanted to err on the side of "too much ease" to avoid a very serious downturn in the economy.

The economic recession that took place lasted only one quarter, the shortest economic recession in U.S. history.

Then, in March 2022, the Federal Reserve entered into a period of quantitative tightening aimed at fighting the inflationary pressures that it had initiated with the Powell-driven campaign to avoid a "worse" recession.

Now, the economy has returned to a period of economic growth that resembles the performance of the U.S. economy in the 2010s.

In terms of the price level, the year-over-year rate of increase in the PCE price index, the one the Fed is primarily interested in...the numbers read as follows: In the second quarter of 2022, the year-over-year rate of increase came in at 6.9 percent, and this was followed by a 3.9 percent rate of increase in 2023, a 2.6 percent rate of increase in 2024, and a 2.4 percent rate of increase in 2025.

The rate of inflation has been trending down toward the Fed's objective of achieving a 2.0 percent rate of growth over time.

So, the performance of the U.S. economy seems to be following a supply-side trajectory as the Federal Reserve produces a "quantitative" approach to monetary policy. One can argue that this "quantitative" effort has been going on since 2009, when Chairman Bernanke initiated the program.

The only interruption to this growth path has been the short but severe recession coming in the year 2020, generated by the COVID-19 pandemic.

The point?

The monetary policy approach initiated by Bernanke has generated a "supply-side" performance in the U.S. economy.

The one...MAJOR...disruption to the operations of the Federal Reserve during this period of time was initiated by a "shock" to the economy that disrupted the performance of the economy...for a very short period of time...but this disruption ended very quickly, and the economy returned to its supply-side path.

Chairman Powell and the current policymakers at the Federal Reserve seem intent upon carrying out this approach.

President Trump seems intent upon returning to a more "demand-driven" monetary approach.

This is the "fight" that is going on.

What can one say to this?

The last sixteen years have seen the introduction of a "new" approach to monetary policy.

The "quantitative" approach to the application of monetary policy seems to allow the economy to grow from the supply side and eliminate policy-caused swings in economic activity.

In this kind of world, businesses can focus on what they do best and reduce the amount of time and resources they use to respond to "business cycle swings" caused by variations in government policies.

Businesses can allocate more time and resources to increase productivity and to innovate into new eras. The digital world and the world of artificial intelligence have really progressed during the past 15 years or so.

A lot of us like the performance that has been achieved and do not want to go back to a focus on interest rate changes and abundant business cycles.

A lot of us like the "Bernanke" approach that has been practiced over the past 15 years or so.

A lot of us like a supply-side approach to the growth of the economy.

A lot of us like it when businesses spend more time on the improvement of productivity and the innovation into new eras of technology and product development.

A lot of us like it when the credit for great performance can be spread around and shared.

data.title: TSPY: The New King Of S&P 500 Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
The TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (NASDAQ:TSPY) has recently passed its first-year milestone with phenomenal results. When I last covered TSPY in February, the fund had only been out for six months with already phenomenal results that had led me to rate the fund as a Buy. Since then, the fund's 0 days-to-expiry ("0DTE") covered call strategy has continued to prove itself during one of the worst markets for selling covered calls earlier this year in April, and has shown itself to be able to match or even beat the S&P 500 in total returns while consistently generating 14%+ distribution yields. That said, it is prudent not to expect TSPY to beat the S&P 500 continuously. The fund is actively-managed, and at the end of the day, no options strategy is successful 100% of the time. Still, I upgrade my rating on TSPY to a Strong Buy and recommend it as a core S&P 500 covered call holding alongside a few other funds.

TSPY's Strategy and Its First Year's Success

TSPY is a 0DTE S&P 500 covered call ETF. What this means is that it holds the S&P 500 as its underlying asset and sells covered calls on the S&P 500 daily. Selling 0DTE options has become a popular strategy in the income ETF space because it can capture a considerable portion of the upside in the underlying asset. And that is what TSPY has done. Although there is inevitable long-term drag compared to the index itself due to the nature of covered calls capping your upside, TSPY has offered similar returns as SPY in the past year, while offering large distributions. According to the TappAlpha website, TSPY has had a 12-month trailing yield of 14.25%. This is phenomenal because other popular competitors like the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE) and Defiance S&P 500 Target Income ETF (SPYT) have yielded more at the cost of total returns or offered better total returns in exchange for a lower payout like the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) and Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX). For reference, XDTE yields 29.74%, SPYT yields 20.85%, SPYI yields 11.92%, and GPIX yields 8.13% at the time of writing this article.

Now, the beauty of TSPY and what has really redefined the covered call income ETF space is the use of active management in options trading. Many covered call funds have traditionally used more passive strategies, where they mechanically sold the same options regardless of market conditions. Some examples are the Global X S&P 500® Covered Call ETF (XYLD), which sells at-the-money ("ATM") call options on the S&P 500 at the same time every month and the iShares S&P 500 BuyWrite ETF (IVVW), which sells the same monthly option as XYLD but 1% out-of-the-money ("OTM") instead. The ProShares S&P 500 High Income ETF (ISPY) is also a great fund to compare TSPY to as it tracks the S&P 500 Daily Covered Call Index via swaps. Without the ability to adjust strike prices and notional values or close-out positions early as needed, these passively-managed covered call ETFs have often underperformed actively-managed funds such as TSPY. The two biggest downsides have been the funds' inability to capture upside effectively and an often highly variable distribution.

Comparatively, a more active options trading strategy generally gives more flexibility to the fund managers to adapt to market conditions, and the funds become particularly suitable for income investors if they adopt target distribution strategies. This is something TSPY has done, where it targets a 14-14.5% distribution yield, according to TappAlpha's CEO, Si Katara, in my conversation with him as well as various interviews on YouTube. Thus, the result is that TSPY's payouts are fairly steady and not only give investors the benefit of having a more predictable income stream to manage their expenses, but also a stronger ability to manage their investing psychology and strategies. When income levels remain the same, falling share prices just become opportunities to grow your income stream faster. And this is especially the case with TSPY because its underlying is the S&P 500 via SPY. It's not the safest investment in the world, with a historical maximum drawdown of 55.2%, but it has never failed to recover to new highs.

The other benefit of active management in options trading is the capability for a fund to improve its trading strategies over time. Earlier this year in April, covered call strategies and 0DTE in particular, faced one of their biggest tests. Due to Trump's initial tariff announcements, the S&P 500 saw its biggest one-day loss since 2020, and the selloff between April 3-4 was among the worst single-day losses in the entire S&P 500 history. However, it was the sudden recovery as a result of Trump's tariff pause that posed as the real test for covered call ETFs. Selling covered calls is a strategy where your underlying NAV has essentially no downside protection, and its upsides are capped. Thus, one of the worst fears for any covered call strategy is for the market to crash and then suddenly recover. TSPY, like some of its 0DTE competitors, suffered in April where performances that once matched or even beaten the S&P 500 lagged behind as the funds failed to capture upside effectively.

TSPY's active approach, however, allowed the fund managers to learn and improve operations. Since April 2025, adjustments have been made to TSPY's prospectus and investment strategies, enabling the fund managers to implement protective measures, such as selling call spreads and buying protective puts, to enhance returns. In my conversation with Si Katara, protective put measures have yet to be executed, but they have made use of call spreads where they sell a call option and buy one further OTM in case the underlying S&P 500 rose quickly in value. The effectiveness of this strategy can be seen in TSPY's sudden rises around early June and July in the previous chart that ultimately led the fund to almost match SPYI's total return performance post-April 2025 despite lagging behind initially. These performances and adaptations are why TSPY has become so appealing. The fund offers some of the best overall total returns among S&P 500 covered call ETFs, with an over 14% distribution yield.

TSPY's Tax Advantages

Additionally, TSPY's tax considerations are relatively favorable for a covered call income ETF. The fund makes use of SPX index options that qualify as 1256 contracts, where 60% of profits are taxed as long-term capital gains and 40% as short-term, regardless of how long the position is held. In TSPY's first Annual Report, about 83% of their distributions were classified as return of capital ("ROC"). This is a tax classification where distributions are not immediately taxed and instead, lower your cost-basis in the ETF. You will then be taxed when you sell your shares or when your cost-basis reaches zero. Compared to certain covered call ETFs, such as those that use synthetic option strategies like XDTE, TSPY holds the underlying S&P 500 itself and will not have to distribute unrealized gains from the underlying S&P 500 due to mark-to-market rules. This allows TSPY to have an overall more balanced fund structure that provides strong income and relative stability in the underlying NAV.

TSPY's Risks

On the topic of NAV, it should be noted that TSPY is not the holy grail where it can always offer both large 14% distributions and share price appreciation. Despite the S&P 500 being up nearly 17%, TSPY's actual share growth has only risen 2.32%. So when the fund is labeled as one that offers both growth and income, the growth part really only refers to generating similar total returns as the underlying index. You should not expect to pull the entirety of TSPY's distribution and see underlying capital growth all the time, especially considering we are in a time when the S&P 500 is at its all-time high. Should the S&P 500 fall and not quickly return to these same levels, you will have an unrealized loss in your TSPY position.

Furthermore, TSPY's distribution target yield is flexible and based on its NAV on a month-to-month basis. This can be seen in how TSPY's April distribution fell alongside its share price, and it makes at least partial reinvestment particularly prudent for income investors to maintain their income stream.

Additionally, 14% does happen to be a number where there is heavy reliance on superior fund management. If you refer to the first chart in this article, you'll notice that TSPY was not the best overall performing S&P 500 covered call ETF in total returns since TSPY's inception. Instead, GPIX was the better performer for good reasons. With a lower dividend yield target of 8.5%, GPIX's covered call strategy allows the fund to open much more of its portfolio for capital appreciation and a greater margin for error. Thus, those who do not require as much income would see overall better results with something like GPIX, where much of the underlying asset can be left for undisturbed growth rather than risking upside every time the fund sells a covered call.

Conclusion

In conclusion, investors in TSPY should not expect the fund to match or outperform the S&P 500. Investors should also not expect the fund to grow its NAV while maintaining 14% distributions consistently. But that doesn't mean you should avoid TSPY. Instead, what you can expect from TSPY is why I rate it a Strong Buy. The fund offers consistent distribution yields of 14% and has shown its strength in capturing the upside in the S&P 500 for a very compelling balance of income and total return. Combine this with TSPY's relative tax efficiency, and it has become a fund that I would recommend as a core S&P 500 covered call ETF holding alongside funds like SPYI and GPIX for active management diversification.

data.title: A Small Cap In A Big Cap's Clothing
data.description:
Remember back in the day when Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL, TSX:AAPL:CA) was the largest company in the world, and you would always hear the phrase, "As goes AAPL, so goes the market"? It was a comment that usually came from bears at times when AAPL’s stock was weak, and they used it as a justification for their bearish sentiment. Reality showed that while AAPL was a large share of the entire market, there were several times when the stock faltered, and the broader market remained strong, and vice versa.

AAPL is no longer the largest company in the world and now trails both Nvidia (NVDA, NVDA:CA)) and Microsoft (MSFT, MSFT:CA) in terms of market cap, which further illustrates the point that the market can rally without AAPL. Just like with AAPL, the market can rally without NVDA and/or MSFT, too. It can also fall at times when those stocks rally. With a market cap of over $3 trillion, AAPL is still a significant part of the S&P 500, but the two can still move independently of each other. The last 12 months are a perfect example.

The chart below compares the performance of AAPL versus the S&P 500 over the last year. While the two traded in similar directions late last year, their paths haven’t been very similar in 2025. AAPL hasn’t closed at a record high since late December, while the S&P 500 is on pace today for its 20th record close of the year. Also, back in February, when the S&P 500 was making new highs, AAPL topped out with a lower high. In the rally off the April lows, as well, the S&P 500 surged, but from mid-May through early August, AAPL moved sideways. As a result of their divergent paths, the correlation between the closing prices of AAPL and the S&P 500 over the last year has been just +0.27.

Where things really get interesting is when you compare the performance of AAPL versus the Russell 2000 over the last year. AAPL may be the third-largest stock in the world, but it has tracked the performance of the Russell 2000 much more closely than the S&P 500. Don’t take our word for it, just look at the chart. While the correlation between AAPL and the S&P 500 over the last year has been just 0.27, with the Russell 2000, it has been +0.66!

Whether AAPL trades more like a small cap than a large cap can be debated, but what about AAPL itself? After trading in a sideways range from late March through early August, AAPL finally broke out of that range just over two weeks ago and broke its downtrend from last December’s high in the process. Immediately after that breakout, the stock tested those breakout points, and provided those levels hold in the days ahead, you probably won’t be hearing that the market can’t rally without AAPL much in the weeks ahead.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Yield Curve Is Steepening
data.description:
Introduction

One of the economic indicators I like to watch is the US Treasury yield curve, which is the plotting of various treasury bills, notes, and bonds by yield.

At the bottom left of the chart is the Federal Funds Rate, set by the Federal Reserve's RRP, which is considered the "overnight rate." Then, we plot each maturity's yield, moving from shortest to longest maturity.

This lets us easily see what yields across the board look like compared to each other, and what rates investors are able to get for various timeframes.

Here is the yield curve from the time of writing in late August 2025:

One of the things to note about this particular curve: it is not normal. It's abnormal for the curve to have the lowest rates at the 2-3yr mark, but that can be explained.

Normally, the yield curve has a positive slope, but ours has a mostly negative slope, until you spike at the longest end.

The Shape of the Yield Curve

Short-term rates are dictated by the Federal Reserve and controlled by their open market operations and ability to pay interest on deposits, which it uses to set benchmark rates.

The Fed has told us, though its last dot plot projection, that they intend to lower rates over the next two years. This means that buyers of 2yr Treasuries today know where rates are headed, and they are pricing those notes accordingly.

The further out on the curve we go, the more speculative the market gets about rates, and the more control it has of the yield itself. The Federal Reserve can only control the short end of the curve; the long end is beholden to market forces, mostly.

Take a look at this comparison, which shows how the yield curve has been moving over time. The blue and red lines (August 2025 and December 2024) share a Fed Funds Rate at 4.25% and the olive, light green, and magenta lines (August 2024, December 2023, and August 2023) all share a Fed Funds Rate at 5.25%. One thing they all have in common?

Their 30yr Treasury rate is lower than our current one, despite the Fed Funds Rate coming down a full 100bp.

In fact, we've seen the long end of the curve defy several cuts from the Fed, going even higher as cuts happen. This was initially blamed on "bond vigilantes," but ultimately, that's just one market force acting on rates. There's more at play than just some market players protesting monetary policy decisions.

Our Steepening Yield Curve

Yield curve steepening is commonly measured through the spread between the 2yr and 10yr Treasuries, which has been trending up consistently since it bottomed two years ago.

In August 2025, it's accelerating, with the 13-week yield falling as the 30-year yield is rising, almost in lock-step. The largest moves were on days with powerful economic releases like the weak jobs report, the hot PPI release, etc.

The steepener is largely a condition created by the dual expectations of rates coming down in the short term and persistent long term inflation.
